African or American?: Conditional Nativism and Black American National Identity *1

Jennifer St Sume University of Maryland, College Park

First version: October 1, 2020 This version: September 29, 2020

Abstract

Black Americans have faced discrimination throughout all of American History. Still, Black Americans report similar levels of national identity to other groups. I argue that Black Americans have a unique disposition toward national identity known as conditional nativism. Conditional nativism is the process of employing racial collectivism to form a positive orientation toward national identity. Using a national sample of respondents from the 2016 Collaborative Multi-racial Postelection Survey (n = 10,146), I explore the role of conditional nativism in shaping Black American national identity. I find that while racial collectivism predicts a positive orientation toward national identity, the degree of racial collectivism produces a negative orientation toward national identity. These results underscore the role of racial collectivism and conditional nativism in Black American orientation toward national identity. 
cmps <- read.dta13("CMPS2016 book ms.dta")

## Warning in Ops.factor(left, right): '<' not meaningful for factors
#National identity index
cmps$c107 <- as.numeric(cmps$c107) #belong
cmps$c108 <- as.numeric(cmps$c108) #respect and value
cmps$c109 <- as.numeric(cmps$c109) #how much do you feel like an outsider in the US
cmps$c110 <- as.numeric(cmps$c110) #excluded

cmps$natid <-(rev(cmps$c107) + rev(cmps$c108) + cmps$c109 + cmps$c110)/4
cmps$natid <- cmps$natid
summary(cmps$natid) #come back to this specification
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   1.000   2.000   2.500   2.385   2.750   4.000
#IVS
cmps$allrace <- cmps$ethnic_quota
cmps$allrace <- fct_drop(cmps$allrace, c("Middle Eastern or Arab", "American Indian/Native American", "Other" ))
cmps$allrace <- revalue(cmps$allrace, c("Hispanic or Latino" = "Latinx", "Black or African American" = "Black",
                        "White Non Hispanic" = "White", "Asian American" = "Asian"))

cmps$allrace <- cmps$allrace
cmps$Black <- cmps$s2_3
cmps$White <- cmps$s2_1
cmps$Latino <- cmps$s2_2
cmps$Asian <- cmps$s2_4

#Controls
cmps$income <-as.numeric(cmps$c383)
cmps$south <- cmps$south
cmps$citizen <-cmps$citizen
cmps$religion <- cmps$c129
cmps$age <- cmps$age; summary(cmps$age)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's 
##   18.00   28.00   38.00   40.63   52.00   98.00      11
cmps$age <- cut_number(cmps$age,10)
cmps$ed <- as.numeric(cmps$c381)
cmps$native <- cmps$s7
cmps$native <- ifelse(cmps$native == "United States", 1, 0)#Note that this does not include Puerto Rico. This is a separate category. Excluded because irrelevant to my theory. 
cmps$native <- as.numeric(cmps$native)
cmps$party <- cmps$c25 
cmps$party <- recode(cmps$party,"c(3,4)= 0 ;c(1,2)= 1")
cmps$party <- as.numeric(cmps$party)
cmps$Dem <- as.numeric(cmps$c25=="Democrat")
cmps$partisan <- cmps$c26
cmps$partisan <- fct_rev(cmps$partisan)
cmps$partisan <- as.numeric(cmps$partisan)-1
cmps$lf <- as.numeric(cmps$c150) -1
cmps$dlf <- as.numeric(fct_rev(cmps$c151))
cmps$c.dlf <- fct_rev(cmps$c151)
cmps$female <- cmps$s3
cmps$female <- ifelse(cmps$female == "Male", 0, 1)
cmps$female <- as.numeric(cmps$female)

Why reproducible research (in R)?

Black Americans are the only racial American group to have been explicitly rendered property and later integrated into the American national identity. Moreover, Black Americans (among other non-White, male, land-owning, and religious individuals) as an underclass of citizens in the United States’ constitution (Shklar 1991). And so the legacy of racist classifications were institutionalized into the American constitution (Acharya, Blackwell, and Sen 2016). What is the relationship between racial and national identity for Black Americans? The answers to this question inform theories of identity, identity management, electoral behavior, and attitude formation. Thus, Black racial identity is a curious puzzle for political psychology and political science alike. This paper explores the unique sociopolitical position of Black Americans in the American superordinate identity I offer a race-based perspective on national identity and American politics. I argue that racial collectivism is a cognitive mechanism that allows individuals to improve an otherwise negative orientation toward national identity into a positive one. This mechanism that shapes an individual’s orientation toward national identity is what I refer to as conditional nativism. I describe the mechanisms of this orientation toward national identity to disentangle the relationship between racial and national identities for Black and other non-White Americans.

On one hand, Black Americans have sufficient right to feel “less” American than their non-Black counterparts from a legacy of exclusion. Additionally, Black Americans are definitively collectivistic (Dawson 1994) which contends with American individualism. Still, Black Americans national identity is a mixed bag. Figure 1 reports orientation toward national identity among four main racial subgroups of American national identity. Among Black Americans, 61% feel strongly that they belong in the United States, 95% felt any sense of Belonging at all. Comparatively, 76% of White Americans feel strongly that they belong to the American national identity while 93% feel. Unsurprisingly, however, Black Americans are most likely to feel strongly like an outsider (7%) excluded from their national identity (7%). In summary, Black Americans as a subgroup feel both positively and negatively toward their national identity.

#National identity index
cmps$belong <- cmps$gg.c107 <- as_factor(cmps$c107)  #belong
cmps$respect <-  cmps$gg.c108 <- as_factor(cmps$c108)  #respect and value
cmps$out <- cmps$gg.c109 <- as_factor(cmps$c109)  #how much do you feel like an outsider in the US
cmps$exc <- cmps$gg.c110 <- as_factor(cmps$c110)  #excluded
cmps$ggnatid <- cmps$natid

b <- ggplot(cmps, aes(allrace, fill = gg.c107))
b <- b + geom_bar(position = "fill") + theme_classic();b

b <- b + labs(title = "National Identity Orientation: Belonging",
              x = "Race", y = "Proportion") + theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
b <- b + scale_fill_grey(start = 0.2, end = 0.8, na.value = "red", name = "Intensity", 
                         labels = c("High", "Moderate", "Low", "None"));b

r <- ggplot(cmps, aes(allrace, fill = gg.c108))
r <- r + geom_bar(position = "fill") + theme_classic();r

r <- r + labs(title = "National Identity Orientation: Respect and Value",
              x = "Race", y = "Proportion") + theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
r <- r + scale_fill_grey(start = 0.2, end = 0.8, na.value = "red", name = "Intensity", 
                         labels = c("High", "Moderate", "Low", "None"));r

ot <- ggplot(cmps, aes(allrace, fill = gg.c109))
ot <- ot + geom_bar(position = "fill") + theme_classic();ot

ot <- ot + labs(title = "National Identity Orientation: Outsider",
              x = "Race", y = "Proportion") + theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
ot <- ot + scale_fill_grey(start = 0.2, end = 0.8, na.value = "red", name = "Intensity", 
                         labels = c("None", "Low","Moderate","High"));ot

e <- ggplot(cmps, aes(allrace, fill = gg.c110))
e <- e + geom_bar(position = "fill") + theme_classic();e

e <- e + labs(title = "National Identity Orientation: Excluded",
              x = "Race", y = "Proportion") + theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))
e <- e + scale_fill_grey(start = 0.2, end = 0.8, na.value = "red", name = "Intensity", 
                         labels = c("None", "Low","Moderate","High"));e

all <-  grid.arrange(b,r,ot,e); all

## TableGrob (2 x 2) "arrange": 4 grobs
##   z     cells    name           grob
## 1 1 (1-1,1-1) arrange gtable[layout]
## 2 2 (1-1,2-2) arrange gtable[layout]
## 3 3 (2-2,1-1) arrange gtable[layout]
## 4 4 (2-2,2-2) arrange gtable[layout]

Figure 1 reports national identity orientation by racial groups. Each dependent variable is a constituent item of the national identity index I use in Model 1. The x-axis reports racial groups while the y-axis reports the proportion of each group that shares the attachment (Collaborative Multi-Racial Post-Election Survey, n = 10,146).

Black Americans form a different orientation toward American national identity I call conditional nativism. I define conditional nativism as the idea that individual attachment to national identity is conditional on their racial group well-being. Thus, if individuals are cued with information about their racial group they are more likely to feel positively toward it as they have not experienced the same level of exclusion from their racial group as their national identity.

The process through which individual use their racial identity to orients Black American national identity is what I refer to as conditional nativism. This disposition is both politically consequential and theoretically imperative to the study of American politics. First, one’s attachment to national identity can predict their political behavior (Dalton 2008; Klandermans 2014; Rosenstone and Hansen 1993; Valenzuela and Michelson 2016a). Black Americans are a cohesive and engaged groups in American politics. Black voter turnout peaked in 2008, with 65.7% of voters turning out for the presidential election (Pew 2017). In 2012, Black Americans surpassed White voter turnout, and 66.6% of Black voters turning out. Second, the lingering uncertainty of the inclusion of Blacks was the impetus for Donald Trump’s birther conspiracy. In 2008, Donald Trump famously challenged the birthplace and citizenship of President Barack Obama to kickstart the birther movement. While much of these attitudes can be attributed to racial attitudes (Tesler 2015), Black American national identity was the object of contention. Thus, how Black Americans perceive their national identity themselves is part of a larger discussion about how Americans as a superordinate identity view Black American national identity.

The story of this paper is as follows. I begin by describing race as the United States’ fundamental cleavage that forged a White-Black dichotomy of American national identity. I then review social identity theory highlighting how individuals manage cross-pressures between conflicting identities. I propose that conditional nativism is a mechanism to alleviate the pressures of competing identity concerns. I use survey data from the 2016 Collaborative Multi-Ethnic Post Election Survey to model Black attachment to national identity. I find that Black Americans who report racial collectivism are more likely to hold negative views toward their national identity than non-Black. Together, this paper suggests a theory for understanding the relationship between racial (subgroup) and national (superordinate group) identities for low status groups.

  • Access: Research is normally funded by taxpayers (researchers are also taxpayers). Hence, it should be freely accessible to everyone without any barriers, e.g., without requiring commercial software. Importantly, researchers from developing countries are even more dependent on free access to knowledge [@Kirsop2005-ro].
  • Reproducability: Even if you have written a study and analyzed the data yourself you will forget what you did after a few months. A fully reproducible setup will help you to trace back your own steps. Obviously, the same is true for other researchers who may want to understand your work and built on it. It may sound like a joke but why not aim for a document that can be used to reproduce your findings in 500 years.
  • Errors: Manual steps in data analysis (e.g., manually copy/pasting values into a table etc.) may introduce errors. R Markdown allows you to automatize such steps and/or avoid them.
  • Revisions: Revising a paper takes much less time if you have all the code you need in one place, i.e., one .rmd file. For instance, if you decide to exclude a subset of your data you simply need to insert one line of your code at the beginning and everything is rebuilt/re-estimated automatically.
install.packages(c('tinytex', 'rmarkdown'))
tinytex::install_tinytex()
  • …install the packages below using the code below [@bookdown1; @bookdown2; @knitr1; @knitr2; @knitr3; @kableextra; @plotly].
install.packages(c("rmarkdown", "knitr", "kableExtra",
                   "stargazer", "plotly", "knitr",
                   "bookdown"))
  • …download the 5 input files I created — paper.rmd, references.bib, data.csv and american-sociological-association.csl — from this folder. Ignore the other files.

  1. *DRAFT in preparation to meet the third-year paper requirement for the Doctoral program in Government and Politics.↩
